Q1: I usually tried to keep things pretty organized, studying around 19 hours a week spread across most days. I'm a visual learner, so I'd create color-coded notes and diagrams from the lecture materials. I'd typically set aside blocks of time in the afternoon after my extracurricular activities, and I found having a rough schedule helped me stay on track without feeling overwhelmed.
Q2: My pattern was mostly regular, though I'd admit deadlines would make me shift things around sometimes. I tried to study a bit each day rather than cramming, but when assignments were due, I'd definitely spend more time focused on those specific tasks. Generally though, I preferred consistency because it kept my stress levels manageable.
Q3: Honestly, I logged in when I knew there was something specific I needed to do—like checking for new assignments or seeing if grades were posted. Sometimes I'd log in to watch a video that was required for that week. I wasn't one to browse around the LMS just for fun; it was more purposeful and task-oriented for me.
Q4: It sort of became routine during the middle of the semester when we had regular weekly tasks. I'd log in maybe once a day to check announcements and see what was due. The gamified tasks actually surprised me and made it a bit more interesting to check in, which I didn't expect. But I wouldn't say it ever felt completely natural—more like a necessary habit.
Q5: The lecture videos were definitely the most useful because I could pause and rewatch parts I didn't understand, and as a visual learner, seeing diagrams and demonstrations really helped. I also found the discussion boards helpful when classmates explained concepts differently than the professor. Those two resources together covered most of what I needed to grasp the material.
Q6: I tended to skip a lot of the supplementary readings and extra resources that weren't directly tied to assignments. It's not that they weren't valuable, but with my extracurriculars and other courses, I had to prioritize. If something wasn't going to be assessed, I usually saved it for later and then never got around to it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to what I'd be tested on or what skills I was building. I also appreciated when activities had some variety or interactive elements—like the gamified tasks, which actually surprised me by being more engaging than I expected. If something felt like busywork, I'd lose interest quickly.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—there was less pressure, which was nice, but it also meant I often deprioritized them. Required activities had a sense of urgency that made me commit to them even when I wasn't in the mood. With optional stuff, I'd think 'I'll do it if I have time,' and usually I didn't make the time.
Q9: Yes, definitely. I downloaded a lot of the slides and videos early in the semester so I could study without needing internet sometimes, especially when I was at extracurricular practice. I also made my own handwritten notes and visual summaries that I'd review constantly. My textbook was another big resource that wouldn't show up in any LMS tracking.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my printed notes or working through problems in my notebook for hours, and none of that would appear online. I'd also study with a friend from class in person, quizzing each other and discussing concepts. The LMS would show nothing during those productive sessions.
Q11: Absolutely, I did this all the time. I'd watch a video once online, maybe take some screenshots of key diagrams, and then study from those offline. I'd also download PDFs of readings and annotate them on my tablet without being connected. It was just more convenient for my schedule and saved data too.
Q12: Around midterms, things got harder because my extracurricular commitments ramped up at the same time. I wasn't burnt out exactly, but my motivation dipped a bit and I had to really push myself to keep up. I remember feeling surprised by how quickly everything piled up, even though I thought I had planned well.
Q13: Fatigue was probably the biggest factor—after a long day with activities and other classes, logging into the LMS felt like another chore. When I was tired, I'd do the bare minimum online and save deeper studying for when I had more energy. Competing responsibilities definitely made me more strategic about what I engaged with on the platform.
Q14: There were times I'd open a video and have it playing while my mind wandered completely elsewhere. I'd technically watch the whole thing but absorb almost nothing, then have to rewatch it later. It's frustrating because the system would show I completed it, but the learning didn't actually happen in that moment.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts sometimes because they required more mental energy than just watching a video. I knew participating was important for my grade, but I'd tell myself I'd do it later when I had something insightful to say. Sometimes I'd wait until the last minute and then rush through them, which wasn't my best work.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main driver for LMS activity. I'd check the calendar, see what was due, and plan my week around those dates. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged with certain materials at all. They kept me accountable even when my motivation was low.
Q17: Feedback definitely motivated me when it was specific and showed me where I could improve. After getting comments on an assignment, I'd go back and review related materials more carefully. But if feedback was delayed too long, I'd already have moved on mentally and it didn't impact my engagement as much.
Q18: The weekly structure helped me know what to expect and plan accordingly. Having clear modules with defined tasks made it easier to pace myself. The gamified elements were a nice surprise—they added some variety that broke up the monotony. Overall, the structure supported my steady approach to studying.
Q19: Having videos and visual materials readily available made engagement easier for someone like me who learns best that way. What made it harder was when there were too many small tasks scattered around—it felt cluttered and I'd sometimes miss things. Clearer organization of required versus optional content would have helped.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for information or trying to understand the platform itself rather than actually studying. They could also be distracted while logged in, or maybe they're anxious and keep checking things without actually completing work. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me in some ways. I performed reasonably well but my LMS engagement was pretty low because I did so much studying offline. If you know what you need to do and work efficiently outside the system, you don't need to be constantly logged in. The grades reflect the learning, not the clicks.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and completed tasks—the basic compliance stuff. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, and whether someone actually understood anything. They track presence but not depth of learning or genuine intellectual engagement.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material and connecting it to what I already know, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm curious enough to dig deeper or when I find myself thinking about course concepts outside of study time. True engagement is mental investment, not just time spent logged in.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied from my own handwritten notes or how often I discussed concepts with classmates in person. It also wouldn't capture that the gamified tasks genuinely surprised me and shifted my attitude a bit—that emotional response matters but isn't tracked. My real engagement happened mostly away from the screen.
